reviews.review_text:
Okay, this is probably one of my top 3 first-person shooters of all time. I’ve beaten it at least 20 times already. Watch daddy platinum Steam verison now

I don’t even know where to begin explaining why I love it so much. It’s one of those games that comes as close to actual “art” as videogames can get, the complete opposite of the soulless sludge a lot of modern games feel like. I also have a lifetime of memories tied to it from replaying it over and over throughout the years.

This is still one of the greatest shooters you can play purely for the story and atmosphere. I’m not going to talk much about the combat, I admit it may be aged. Either way, after replaying this game so many times, even the hardest difficulty without Vita-Chambers stopped being even a little challenging for me halfway through.

It’s the story and atmosphere that matters. The unbelievably unique underwater Art Deco city. Where else have you seen something like this? But for me, the game also turns into this weird immersive looter simulator where you crawl through the ruins of a once-luxurious city, slaughtering its decadent inhabitants, looting everything not nailed to the floor, smashing every vending machine, drinking every bottle of alcohol you find, and overdosing on plasmids beyond human comprehension. And when old-timey music starts playing from a jukebox while I’m standing there chugging my tenth bottle of Arcadia Merlot surrounded by burning splicer corpses, I experience absolute bliss.

This game is pure visual and atmospheric ecstasy, and somehow it still remains one even on the twentieth playthrough.

If you’re from a newer generation trying it for the first time today, give it a little time to warm up. Not much. Once it does, you won’t be able to stop.
Timeless classic, looks and plays better then many modern games.

They added a binding arbitration clause in the EULA at https://www.take2games.com/legal/en-US/ so in effect they stole my game from me since they changed the terms of the sale after everything was said and done. More and more game companies are doing this and I would highly recommend installing what games you can before binding arbitration EULA's are everywhere.
Tbh the story and lore is awesome but the gameplay is repetitive and not fun

Before starting the game, I thought I won't like it but now I see it was ahead of its time when initially released. Considering all aspects, I enjoyed it even in year 2026. It was good and definitely better than BioShock 2. Didn't find any crash issues like BioShock 2 as well.

It's a pretty fun and entertaining game to play. It's a simple FPS with a pretty good story; even though I've heard complaints about the remaster, I haven't had any issues with it. 

I really liked the game’s atmosphere, since it’s something you don’t find in newer games, and the characters are well developed. The Plasmids aren’t bad, but I didn’t end up using them much since the weapons were already doing the job. Overall, it’s an all-time classic that I highly recommend for both new and experienced gamers.

A man chooses, a slave obeys.
Bioshock
Genre: Immersive Sim FPS
Tags: Rapture - Player Freedom - Atmosphere
	Bioshock is a masterpiece in game design. Plain and simple. The Irrational Games project led by the now legendary Ken Levine is precise in its length, certain in its aesthetic, and confident in its depth (no pun intended). It delivers early halo level enemy AI, a profound message about agency in the face of evil, and peanut butter thick atmosphere that is on the level of a Fallout or Dishonored. Bioshock is a shining example of what the immersive sim genre once was and can still be with the right dev team. If anything, playing through Bioshock has made me sick for a game with this kind of drum head tight execution and certainty in vision. I‘ve asked myself many times why Levine’s upcoming game, Judas, is still so anticipated after multiple delays and nearly a decade-long development cycle. Now I know why.
	I don’t intend to spend much time on the story, but I would be remiss not to at least touch on what many consider to be the standout aspect of this game, though I disagree with that assertion. Bioshock begins with a plane crash into an unknown patch of the Atlantic ocean in 1960. World War 2 has concluded in canon and the geo-politics of the time are still in play. Your player character, Jack, swims a suspiciously short distance after the crash to a towering art deco monolith with an interior that leads him into a sprawling city on the bottom of the ocean, Rapture. Though operational, if you squint hard enough, it is clear from the get that an insidious force has corrupted the minds of Rapture’s denizens as they have a newfound hobby of murdering each other. It doesn’t take long for the classic voice in your head via radio trope character, a man named Atlas, to talk you through your initial encounter and wave of confusion. The city has been driven mad through the unfettered consumption of the DNA altering substance ADAM that allows for people to indulge in powers called Plasmids. Telekinesis, flames from your fingertips, the control of swarms of bees; plasmids offer a plethora of perks perfect to lose your mind for. ADAM also has a psychotropic effect that allows for users to have a hivemind-like connection and even experience each other's memories. Now that you have descended, you’re tasked with heading deeper and helping Atlas save his family from the city and its creator Andrew Ryan. What follows is a rapidly developing and unpredictable journey through Rapture. I literally can’t say more than that or I will give away revelations that, no matter how small, never let you get too comfortable in this watery grave.
	What I can expound upon is one of my favorite parts of the game: the now famous Big Daddy/Little Sister characters that Bioshock has become famous for. Through procedures best left to the imagination, little girls in Rapture were indoctrinated and genetically modified into glowing eyed husks with one goal: to produce and gather ADAM. You see, ADAM is a heavily sought after and limited resource that doesn't leave the body when someone dies. It's also highly addictive and fought over by the remaining Rapture citizens. That means these little buggers are programmed to walk around and extract any ADAM left over in the bodies of the fallen. They still skip around and complain like normal children, but you are led to believe from the beginning that they are anything but. ADAM is also fittingly used for the player's upgrades, a series of plasmid powers and passives that can be assigned to a limited number of slots that allows you to express your preferred playstyle. I was always keen on finding the heaviest or most explosive item in the room and hurling it at my enemies with the telekinesis plasmid. “Hunting down little girls is easy”, you say. First of all, whoa there. Second of all, you don’t know about Big Daddies yet. These massive, whale calling, diving suit cyborgs were originally intended to carry out maintenance and construction in the watery depths of Rapture’s undeveloped land. Now, they have been reprogrammed to protect little sisters at all costs. The character oozes from these wandering NPCs. Each little sister looks unbelievably tired, dawning large bags under their eyes, while their Big Daddies stomp and wail through water tight corridors making their locations known like a sad Mr. X from Resident Evil 2. These “enemy” types lead to the most interesting dilemma of the game. First, are you willing to take on a Big Daddy for that sweet, sweet ADAM. These hulks neutrally march around the multitude of semi-linear maps at your disposal with their little sisters unless provoked, so you must ask yourself if its the right time to ♥♥♥♥ around and find out. Second is the decision on what to do with the little sisters once the big guy has fallen. Do you end the little ♥♥♥♥ and take a larger yield of ADAM, or do you deprogram the little one for less ADAM but a delayed, unspecified reward from the Little Sister’s reluctant creator, Dr. Brigid Tenenbaum. This decision may be harder than it sounds given your incomplete knowledge of the situation, but it's one of the few you actually get to make.
	This give and take is additive to the growing uncertainty with each step deeper into the art deco nightmare that is Rapture. That uncertainty is cut with the growing confidence you have in dealing with this world through gameplay. What starts out as a modest kit including a monkey wrench and simple electric bolt plasmid turns into a sandbox of tools and weapons you can use in clever ways to progress. I found myself fond of freezing people in place with an ice plasmid and gingerly walking up to their personal space to deliver a shell from my shotgun by hand. There was a moment midway through the game where a space I walked into was obviously about to devolve into a deluge of ADAM crazed psychos, or as the game calls them Splicers. With this instinct, I was able to back track a short distance and transport a collection of explosive objects and store them in the room. When the doors locked behind me and the enemies poured in, I was delighted to fling each and every red barrel I had at my disposal. I’m also reminded of an instance where I had drone-like enemies sic’ed on me after triggering an alarm. After zapping them out of commission, I was able to hack the fallen security bots and use their hovering firepower to my advantage. This is a technique I was able to take further into the game as this security mechanic persists. These strategies were constantly challenged by an increasingly impressive set of enemy behaviors. When I’d bring an enemy to near death, they’d scramble to find one of many healing stations on the wall that are also at the player's disposal. In the next moment, I’d light a splicer on fire only to watch him comically belly flop into the nearest puddle to put out the flames. This kind of reactivity fills you with the sense that whatever clever ♥♥♥♥ you may dream up, the game likely has an answer. Water cooler moments are often what makes a game, but Bioshock makes a habit out of placing these opportunities around every ornate corner.
	Games from this era and before always impress me with their economy of assets and mechanics. Irrational Games was still an independent studio at the time of developing Bioshock. They had found great critical success in classics like System Shock 2 and SWAT 4, but neither of these games had sold exceptionally well.  Eventually, under great stress and time constraints, Bioshock found its publisher in 2K and released in 2007. This time, Irrational had a game that was reviewed highly and sold like hot cakes. By 2008, Bioshock had sold more than 2 million copies and launched Ken and the team into the atmosphere. It’s said that it's a miracle that any game exists, but Bioshock feels like true divine intervention. All of this has been a long winded way for me to ask you, would you kindly play Bioshock?

[h1]Bioshock's gameplay shows its age but even in 2026, it is a MUST play.[/h1]

I never got the chance to play Bioshock as a kid so getting to it now was a no brainer and oh boy do I understand why this was considered an instant classic! The atmosphere, the story and lore, everything is just so intriguing and well executed. The collection with all 3 games often goes on sale so, would you kindly do yourself a favor and play this game?

Bioshock deserves better. If you've never played it before, just buy the non-remastered that's where you'll get the 10/10 experience the game is known for.

It's very hard to recommend this game. I love Bioshock, it was one of the first games I played as a teenager where I felt truly connected and immersed with the world and it's story. The story is incredible, the setting even more so and to put it bluntly this game in and of itself is an achievement on it's own. With all that said, this remaster and it's lack of support is truly heartbreaking to say the least. While it is essentially a remaster with no changes to it's core gameplay, environment, or story it runs incredibly poorly and suffers from frequent crashes that I found was very common for other players. The director's commentary and challenge modes are a lot of fun and are very creative, but in all honesty I'd recommend just buying the original. It's not just a time capsule of the game's original state but it just runs much better and visually still holds up due to it's beautiful art direction. Such a shame too, as like the first sentence says, this franchise deserved better than this.

Lovely experience, and left me enthralled every twist and turn. Great gunplay and decently challenging. I definitely didn't use everything in my arsenal but it was a good experience. Highly recommended.
10/10

Note: This spoiler free review focuses on BioShock as a game rather than the remaster’s performance and compatibility. Those points are mentioned near the end, but are not a primary factor.

[i] “I chose something different. I chose the impossible. I chose... Rapture.” [/i]

[h3] Gameplay: [/h3]

[i] “There's an arms race on here in Rapture, but it's not about who can build the best guns and the biggest bombs. It's about who can become less of a man and more of a monster…” [/i]

Calling BioShock a first person shooter feels incomplete. Technically it is, but it offers far more freedom of approach than most games in the genre.
Rapture’s enemies, the “Splicers”, come in many forms: ranged fighters, demolitions experts, acrobatic slashers, brutish pipe wielders and elemental variants that punish particular combat styles. Some enlist security bots or attack from walls and ceilings. Alongside them are Rapture’s own defences: turrets, cameras and aforementioned drones that force you to adapt.
Combat becomes truly interesting through Plasmids. Alongside traditional weapons, you gain powers fuelled by EVE that let you electrocute enemies, ignite environments, turn security against foes or unleash swarms of bees. Experimenting with Plasmids, ammo types and the environment creates encounters that feel dynamic and reactive.
A Big Daddy wandering near Splicers, a turret and a camera can be approached in countless ways. Fight directly, hack security, manipulate enemies into fighting each other or turn the environment into a trap. BioShock constantly rewards experimentation.
Gene Tonics further customise gameplay by enhancing hacking, combat, looting and exploration. By the late game they become invaluable, allowing players to shape their own playstyle.
All of these mechanics culminate in encounters with BioShock’s iconic Big Daddies. These hulking protectors guard Little Sisters, who harvest ADAM — the resource used to upgrade Plasmids, health and abilities. Fighting a Big Daddy demands proper use of everything the game has taught you: traps, positioning, elemental effects and preparation. They remain some of the game’s most memorable encounters, especially on higher difficulties.

[h3] Visuals / Environments: [/h3]

[i] “It was not impossible to build Rapture at the bottom of the sea. It was impossible to build it anywhere else.” [/i]

From its opening moments, BioShock establishes an incredible level of visual and thematic consistency. The art deco presentation, signage, propaganda and environmental storytelling all feel meticulously crafted. Every room, hallway and advertisement tells part of Rapture’s story.
The city itself feels alive. Through windows you see neon skylines, sea life and distant Big Daddies roaming tunnels. Leaking pipes create puddles you can electrify, while nearby notes and audio diaries explain the politics and failures behind the damage. Rapture feels less like a backdrop and more like the game’s true main character.
Despite the undersea setting, environments remain varied: metro stations, gardens, factories, residential districts, scientific facilities and harbours all feel distinct. Exploration is heavily rewarded through hidden routes, vents, locked rooms and alternate paths that tie directly into your chosen abilities and upgrades.
Even menus and upgrade stations are integrated into the world’s retro-futuristic aesthetic. Weapons evolve visually as they’re upgraded, blending prohibition-era designs with Rapture’s dieselpunk style. The attention to detail is exceptional.
BioShock also leans heavily into horror. Lighting, enemy ambushes, mutilated faces and unsettling audio create constant tension. The Mature rating feels entirely earned.
The sound design is stellar. Weapons sound heavy and mechanical, drones buzz menacingly and environmental audio constantly reinforces the atmosphere. Splicers mutter, sing and scream through corridors while Big Daddies thunder through the halls. Combined with strong voice acting and music, the audio does an enormous amount of work in immersing the player.

[h3] Story / Characters [/h3]

[i] “This city is not built with concrete and steel… it is built with ideas.” [/i]

BioShock’s story is delivered through both direct interactions and environmental storytelling. Audio diaries scattered throughout Rapture flesh out its citizens, politics and downfall. Piecing together timelines and character arcs becomes one of the game’s greatest strengths.
These recordings are optional, but they add enormous depth to the world. Some contain useful gameplay information, while others exist purely to expand the setting and its themes.
The player is eased through Rapture by a man named Atlas via shortwave radio. Other characters eventually contact you the same way, including Andrew Ryan himself. Without spoiling anything, the game’s cast and narrative absolutely live up to the quality of the setting.
The Splicers themselves reinforce the world building. Their ramblings reveal loneliness, fanaticism and societal collapse. BioShock constantly rewards players who pay attention to environmental details and contextual storytelling rather than relying purely on exposition.
Performance
I played the original BioShock on Xbox years ago and revisited the remaster on Steam. The remaster has a reputation for technical issues, but personally I encountered no crashes or major performance problems once a few quick fixes were made.
I experienced:
* A brightness slider issue
* FPS dips in fullscreen
* Brief audio cutouts when changing outputs
All were easily solved and never returned.
Graphically, the remaster is modest but effective. Some effects and character models have aged, but the environments, physics and atmosphere still hold up remarkably well for a 2007 release.
One annoyance was inconsistent Splicer voice volume. Enemies sometimes sounded far away when they were nearby and vice versa, occasionally drowning out audio diaries.

[h3] Summary [/h3]

[i] “Who can forget their first view of the city? Amazing what a man can create once he gets government and God off his back.” [/i]

Some mechanics lose their appeal over time, particularly the hacking minigame, which becomes repetitive late game. Vita-Chambers also reduce tension since death carries little punishment. Asset reuse is noticeable in some character models as well.
Even so, these issues feel minor compared to the scale and execution of everything BioShock achieves.
BioShock remains one of the best games I’ve ever played. Its gameplay variety, unforgettable setting, environmental storytelling, themes and atmosphere still stand out today. It was ahead of its time in 2007 and, even in 2025, still surpasses many modern games in these areas.
Not only do I recommend it to anyone who hasn’t played it, I recommend revisiting it if it’s been years since your last playthrough. It’s a reminder of what makes truly great games memorable.

Amazing gameplay, still holds up after 19 years, its strategic fast and has a lot of variety.
and Don't even get me started on the world of BIOSHOCK.
Rapture isn't just the backdrop of this game its integrated into everything smoothly whether its the map design, environmental storytelling or the characters, everything feels organic yet not forced unlike some other games..

One of the best games I have ever played.

The story is easily one of BioShock’s strongest qualities. It’s engaging, well-paced, and surprisingly deep without ever becoming confusing. At no point was I lost or unsure of what was happening, which is something many story-heavy games struggle with. In my opinion, it has one of the greatest stories ever written in gaming.

The gameplay is also fantastic. The variety of weapons, ranging from melee to long-range combat, keeps encounters fresh throughout the entire game. Plasmids essentially act like spells, and combining them with different weapons allows for a lot of creativity in combat. Being able to approach enemies in your own way makes every encounter feel more dynamic.

The enemy variety also helps prevent the game from becoming repetitive. Many games eventually hit a wall where the gameplay loop starts to feel stale, but BioShock never reached that point for me. Across multiple playthroughs, I was never bored or tired of the experience.

Truly a surreal game, and one I would recommend to everyone.

BioShock is a game that definitely proves that games can, in fact, be art. Just by playing, you can absolutely see the love that Irrational Games put into the game. Proven by its writing, art style, and music alone. The only reason this isn't a 5 is because of the very black and white moral dilemma that the gameplay and story circle's around. Freeing or Harvesting the little sisters is for anyone who wants to take the choices seriously. A very obvious and stupid moral decision that only proves to make the atmosphere and writing much more watered down then the original intentions of the devs. Though keep in mind. That was not entirely their fault. Corporate entities wanted them to make the choice simpler so as not to offend or push the game to a higher age rating. 

Either way. BioShock is almost a masterpiece.

Bioshock is one of the most talked about games in the internet for a great reason
It's an amazing Shooter, combining fun and dynamic game mechanics which are extremely fun to interact with, But it's ambiance and setting singlehandedly overshadow nearly every other FPS in the market.
Rapture is an amazing location, Ranging from the Lush-Green Arcadia, to the Asylum-White of Doctor's Hall and the Fiery Orange of Hephaestus, This game is an amazing experience even nearly 20 years after it's release.
Light 10/10.
Good gameplay, EXTREMELY repetitive though. The story is good. I can’t say much since I had the best part of it ruined for me.
its pretty good and the graphics are amazing

It's not as mechanically sophisticated like System Shock 2 but compromises that for a more interesting world, villain and story

♥♥♥♥ i cannot write reviews for the life of me i can't be constructive

Honestly? Didn't age super well. I have fond memories of it, but it's a game I enjoy thinking about more than playing. The first half of it is... okay. The second half just throws wave after wave of the same, like, four incredibly tanky enemies to waste your ammo while you go on random fetch quests to extend the playtime. I genuinely love this game's story and setting, but every time I play it I like it less.

Good game. Only issue is it takes minute for the "Connecting......." window to finally finish just for me to start the game. 

Review will change after I finish the game

Great atmosphere, characters and story.

Although, the gameplay is getting tired by the end of the game.
I shiver like a ♥♥♥♥♥ whenever I play this game. Not recommended for ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ like myself.
Great Story , amazing visuals and great gameplay. Bought the trilogy remake for 10 bucks great value

All achivements gotten, it's fun but clearly aged. Story was surprisingly interesting and near the end it had me on the edge of my seat. Gameplay is fun, enemies are dumb as heck but fun enough to take out so it's not too big of a deal. Environmental story telling and environments in general are where this game shines most. Worth playing and during a sale it costs so little you'd be doing yourself a disservice not getting it

every thing in this game is amazing ive never played something so engaging and beautiful it was from a era of gaming where gaming was at its peak dont get me wrong there are bugs and glitches but it is so fun and cool in so many aspects you must try it for yourself

Not crazy about the story, like the variety of plasmids, simplicity of weapon/plasmid selection, controller support is definitely a selling point, exploiting the gambling machines by saving/reloading is fun.  At the most difficult settings the start of the game makes it very save intensive.  Once you progress, saving isn't as vital unless necessary.

I played Bioshock because it was on sale and I had heard of how impactful it was for all Sci-Fi that came after and I can understand why. It has a lot of, for the most part, well done concepts. 

The enemy design I really liked! I'm sure fans of the game can guess the two I love most but as a further hint ones on the cover of the game. Overall the game was fun, felt good to play, and had an interesting story.

I'd give it a 9/10. I loved most of the game but there were a few touch points, like a lot of genetics and eugenics stuff, that I didn't love. It seems to be that the game was trying to pant them in a bad light but it also felt like it was not making it as clear it could have. Overall it did not take away much from my game but it is something that gives me hesitancy to give it a 10/10.

Everyone can agree that Bioshock is an amazing game, I doubt you would be here if you hadn't already heard of how good it was. And it really is an amazing game. But it's also kinda old, so it doesn't run well on modern systems. I was able to get mine to work by turning off the Steam Overlay and closing Discord. Basically, make sure that no apps that have overlays that show you stuff during games is on, and it should be able to go in full screen, at least for me. Other than that, the game works as well as it ever did, and Bioshock is considered a classic for good reason.

It's good, but so so so buggy. I get a notification that I haven't gotten all of the little sisters in the area and they are nowhere to be found. I restart the entire playthrough so I can get all of the atom... but in one of the first elevators I somehow clip into the wall and get stuck. Gonna give it one more go but, all the exploring and looting every nook and cranny I did is time wasted.

Playing through BioShock again has cemented it as one of my favorite single player fps series. The introduction into Rapture is great and sets the tone for the rest of the game. The gameplay holds up well enough but the narrative is definitely a strongpoint with the atmosphere holding everything together. The Big Daddy is iconic and I have a personal love for the vending machines and it's circus of values. Many systems come together to make Rapture feel alive.

4/5.

I played this game on Xbox originally so don't take my playtime at face value. Bioshock is an extremely awesome classic shooter. The atmosphere is wicked. The shooting definitely feels a tad dated, though.

I Can't believe I missed out on BioShock all these years. I finally played it, and now I completely understand why it's considered a classic. The atmosphere, story, characters, and world-building are on another level. Rapture is one of the most memorable settings I've ever experienced in a game.

Even today, the graphics, audio, and gameplay hold up surprisingly well. It's not often a game lives up to the hype after so many years, but BioShock absolutely does. If you're like me and somehow never got around to playing it, do yourself a favour and give it a shot. Bloody amazing game from start to finish.
I'm having so much fun with this that I didn't even vote during the presidential election in this country. I also stopped going to the gym. Send help.

I wish I played this game earlier, this is honestly one of the greatest video games I have ever played. The story is amazing, the combat system is awesome and the environment is the single best environment out of any video game ever made.
amazing game and amazing story i think everyone should try bio shock atleast once might 100% the game its that good
There is currently a bug that when you exit the game your difficulty settings reset to medium with vita chambers on, mildly annoying, 10 year old game plus its a remaster it should have been fixed

Bioshock is an incredible game, so is Bioshock 2. one of the best video games ever made.
For as much as bioshock remastered improves, it also ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ SUCKS as a remaster. so many problems, so many random ass issues. 2K absolutely sucks and this remaster is blatant evidence of it.
A true classic. The gunplay might be sub-par, but the game as a whole is worth it. Play it!

For a game that was made way back in 07', this piece of entertainment has been nothing but non-stop memories of fun and nostalgia. If you are a fan of looking for loot, gaining powers and diving into a world of story and lore with tons of adventure mixed in with a little fear. This game is absolute in those regards; in the field of artistic expression this game is also up there with some of the best in terms of atmosphere and immersion in the world of Rapture. Overall, this game is an all time banger!
unironically a little scary sometimes, i dont get scared though so it doesnt affect me

A good game for its time, but the storytelling falls a bit flat; you have to learn the story by reading things around the environment. The environment design and atmosphere are very successful, and the game mechanics are adequate for that period, but the missions we do in the game are a bit plain and boring.
Great gameplay and story. Save the little sister!
this game is an actual masterpiece, no other game comes close to the visual style and setting of this beautiful game.
Classic horror game. FPS with some gore and sci-fi nautical fun. Really miss this franchise.
very good narrative, elements fit story line, and surprises.
This game... you're not ready for it. To me Bioshock Remastered is really just freedom.

Great game, reminds me abit of Titan fall with the weapon types and story line. In game such as these Im used to running so shift is a common mistake I make as I forget that that isn't possible. The hacking puzzles are nice and a change of pace. Over all positve experience :)
yes would 100% recommend because it's the best story mode games to complete my first time trying this type of story mode game out about to do the 2nd bioshock

its good the story is good game play is descent but i must warn you all to save every 15 to 20 min their is a autosave but thats only when u go to a new area just save every now and then and your golden would recommend

even in 2026, the gameplay, mechanics, arsenal, story, and style are unique. This has the same feeling of playing games like half life 2, fallout, and high on life for the first time. Even epstien is in here. will also run on a complete potato of a computer
i went into this masterpiece only knowing it was a good game that a lot of people liked and after finishing it I can recommend it more 10/10

Bought the game knowing nothing about it other than looking at the game page and my friends liked it, so I thought it was going to be a horror type game. Going in blind definitely made the experience better. My favorite part of the game was probably the builds and all the different ways you could play. It kept the gameplay fun and interesting the whole time.
10/10 all time favorite game. I was concerned with it running correctly on my PC after all the comments but it was flawless for me. Not a single crash in my entire playthrough.

Oldie but a goodie, sure the grahics are a lil dated from the 2007 era, but the concept, story, atmosphere and art is amazing, weird crashes happen apparently due to a compatibility layer even on windows (currently on win 11 for now) but otherswise, ive missed playing this game, good to be back.
still a great game after all these years had some crashing issues with the games engine but other then that i loved the game as much as i did when it was on the 360
Playing on my New m4 Macbook Air. First time playing a game like this on M&K. Amazing game with a chilling atmosphere and very great story.

I loved this game!

The story/atmosphere/characters are fantastic.

I felt that the combat of Bioshock 2 was a lot better, but that's a relatively minor quibble.

This game crashes. There is no autosave either. When it crashed and I had about two hours of playthrough to go back and do, I just called it right there lol. The game itself is good but I have seen a lot of other PC players complain about crashing on this one.
Incredible world building and story telling while having very impressive gameplay for a game made almost 20 years ago

I'm only giving this game a good review on the bias that it is, in fact, Bioshock 1. However, heed my warning; holy mother of all things great in the world, does this game need community patches. Texture bugs, unbalanced audio, achievements not working properly, and the settings changing every time you boot up the game, it's all here in one remastered package!
I'll be honest, if it was not for the community caring for the game more than the slimes masquerading as humans at 2K, this review would have been bad. But if you are willing to spend a good few minutes downloading patches and fixing what 2K refuses to touch, then the experience is worth it.
Also, Infinite isn't canon and never will be.
If youre lookin for sum good story, backgrond, atmosphere and grafic this game are pretty good. but since this is an old game the mechanic of gameplay are old

I used to be so scared of this game when I was a kid haha. Now I’m all grown up and I decided to give it another try. What an amazing game! What an absolutely wonderous adventure! Loved the gameplay. Loved the story. Loved the environment. Loved the enemies. Loved the combat system. Gotta give another shout-out to the story and antagonists. All around an amazing game. There is some replay value, however there’s not many story-related decisions that affect gameplay. It would be mostly about reexperiencing the game, which is actually pretty worth it because you have all the knowledge you didn’t have when going in blind like I did. Great game, would definitely recommend.

The original Bioshock is a classic and easily one of the best first person RPG shooters of its time, as System Shock (2) was before it. And while it is still quite enjoyable to play and visually still very appealing, its age does show its limits 20 or so years later with titles like Prey and Cyberpunk having taken the genre to new heights both visually and technically within those two decades. We basically have been spoiled with everything that is possible in gaming now that older games start feeling limited gameplay wise. It doesn't make the game bad, but I do think it is important to mention and realise that some things are best experienced in the time they are created.

So, while I do recommend Bioshock Remastered to anyone interested in atmospheric, narrative driven RPG shooters, I do so cautiously and with appreciation for the franchise and what it did for gaming. 


Also, I had little to no issues getting the remastered version to run on a modern system. I didn't experience any crashes or performance issues and technical issues that were present seem to have mostly been patched out by now. And if you do run in to any, like with the sound or UI, they can easily be fixed or modded.
But whatever you do, stay away from the non-remastered version.

First off, the game's setting is incredible. The theme is executed perfectly. The story is gripping and quite interesting. Who would have thought of something like Rapture? Incredible.

However, the gunplay doesn't feel great. Some weapons have way too much recoil. You constantly have to do the same repetitive and boring hacking minigames. Also, towards the end of the game, you end up with so many weapons and abilities that I started getting confused about what buttons to press. Having a few options and using them well is sometimes leaps and bounds better than having too many. Unfortunately, BioShock isn't like that. While Plasmids are fun, I have to admit they get boring really fast. Especially when you're trying to use certain ones, you end up taking constant damage and regretting even trying to use the ability in the first place.

It's a semi-open world but ultimately a linear game. Exploring every corner to get better loot is a nice and enjoyable touch.

When I started this game, my expectation was for it to be something like Half-Life. I was expecting something truly groundbreaking. Instead, it felt more like the Dishonored before Dishonored. I love that game too, but here, the abilities and weapons just get too jumbled up. I don't think I am curious about the rest of the series, and I feel like I finally understand why this franchise went under.

I just finished BioShock in under 10 hours, mostly cuz i already played it as a kid. that game really ♥♥♥♥♥♥ me up back then. one of the first ones that actually stayed in my head for years. wasn’t scary like horror, but the vibe just felt wrong. heavy. like something was always off.
playing it again now at 23, Rapture still gets to me. this underwater city that was supposed to be perfect but completely fell apart. walking through those flooded broken halls — dead silent one minute, total chaos the next — you can feel how bad it all went wrong. the lights, the posters, the audio logs… it all sinks in deep. depressing as hell but i couldn’t look away.
the twist still hit even though i knew it was coming. the way it flips everything you did… made me rethink the whole game.
the ending felt heavier this time too. not some big dramatic thing, just a quiet realization about choice and what you actually become. sat with me more than i thought it would.
idk, BioShock is still insane. the atmosphere and the world are so ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ solid. replaying it just reminded me why it stuck with me all these years. easily one of the best.

BioShock is one of those games that genuinely changed what shooters could be, and it still feels remarkable to play today. The descent into Rapture in those opening minutes is one of the most iconic openings in gaming, and the entire city stands as a brilliant deconstruction of Ayn Rand's Objectivism taken to its logical conclusion. Andrew Ryan's empire of "great men" rotting from the inside is one of the sharpest pieces of philosophical worldbuilding any game has ever pulled off. The !!!!spoiler redacted!!!! twist still hits, the Big Daddies and Little Sisters are unforgettable, and the plasmid-and-gun combat felt genuinely revolutionary at the time. An all-time classic.

Came here to experience a legendary classic. However, I spent two hours troubleshooting crashes.
Watched the plane crash intro 8 times. Tried every “fix” people on the forum and ChatGPT suggested: windowed mode, admin mode, launcher skip, overlays off, input language changes, config edits… and the game still crashes at the very first save point before I can even properly enter Rapture.
This is not just a few technical issues. The game is fundamentally broken on modern systems.
I genuinely wanted to enjoy and love this game, but instead I wasted 2 hours fighting 2007 PC compatibility problems. Lesson learned: always read the reviews before buying old PC classics.

I would say don't judge the game until you have reached the ending. I know the fighting might seem redundant and it can feel boring but this game was competing with COD in 2007. The point was to divert the fast pace shooting meta into a strategic more enjoyable and diversified experience. You can change how make hacking easier or just destroy the cameras with the wrench. It's not trying to be a shooter, but it doesn't mind if you are one. The story is beautiful, the art deco style is done with attention and the splicers design from Nazi patient facial reconstruction is just the tip of the iceberg in the layers of detail that are subliminally placed all around the story of rapture

[h3] 100% Achievement Review: A quick review based on my experience completing all the (available) achievements. [/h3]

[u] [url=https://store.steampowered.com/curator/45897386/] Feel free to follow my curator for more 100% reviews! [/url] [/u]

Title: Bioshock Remastered

Summary: Atmospheric fps game

Personal notes: Bioshock takes place in the pretty yet haunting underwater city of Rapture, a collapsed utopia deep under the sea. You are Jack, a young man who after an "unfortunate" accident is drawn into the forgotten city. You are tasked with surviving all the while uncovering what truly happened to Rapture and it's residents. I played the first two Bioshock games on the xbox growing up but skipped Infinite and decided to go back and play through all three and honestly, I had a blast. The gameplay is fun, mixing the traditional fps experience with genetic abilities called plasmids, giving you powers such as fire, electricity or ice. This combined with a fairly in-depth upgrade system lets you approach combat in many different ways. While the shooting is a little dated nowadays, I still had a good time mixing and matching the guns and the plasmids alike. The world & level design are easily the strongest part of this game IMO. Rapture is iconic and for good reason, exploring this fallen city and slowly piecing together what happened through its visuals and environmental storytelling is still amazing to this day. The storytelling is another very strong aspect. Without spoiling too much as I myself would love another blind playthrough... the mystery throughout and delivery on key moments were great 20 years ago and they are great today. The audio is also very well done, the ambient sound design is immersive and the soundtrack matches the unsettling and isolated city of Rapture to a tee. The voice acting while minimal is also quite strong. Visually this game still has a very distinct look that holds up fairly well. The textures and animations are dated now but the design of Rapture itself creates an atmosphere that still feels unique today. The only down side I can think of is that some things are a little repetitive, such as enemy variation or taking photos of everyone multiple times for an achievement but otherwise I enjoyed the rest. Achievement-wise, most are related to story progression but you do have a few things to keep in mind such as collecting all the audio logs or beating the game on the hardest difficulty without vita-chambers. Nothing is too crazy though. All things considered, Bioshock is an amazing game that continues to stand the test of time. You can tell it's not modern but the experience itself is something special. Rapture itself does a lot of the heavy lifting but the strong storytelling, atmosphere and design choices all combine to make the whole experience standout. I would easily recommend this to anyone who enjoys story-driven games, as well as any atmospheric shooter fans. 

Time it took to complete: 21hrs

Most difficult achievement(s):
- Brass Balls (Complete the game on Hard difficulty without using a Vita-chamber)
- Historian (Find every audio diary)
- I Chose the Impossible (Complete the game on Survivor difficulty without using a Vita-chamber)

Difficulty rating: 
★★★★★★☆☆☆☆ 6/10

Overall rating: 
★★★★★★★★★★ 10/10
